data.title: What Scares The S&P 500?
data.description:
By Roderick Mackenzie & Tom Becker

Pay attention to macro news

The types of macro events driving markets and dominating financial news and broker reports shift over time. Some periods are dominated by Federal Reserve meeting days, others by Consumer Price Index (CPI) or Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP), or even election days. We can quantify such shifts in attention by using index options data and looking at implied equity index moves.

Trading volumes in single-day S&P 500 options have been growing and, by linking them to macro news release dates, we can better understand shifts in how important the market believes these releases are for the broad equity market. The visual below plots the time series of event risk scores in recent years. As has been the case since the beginning of the monetary hiking cycle in 2022, Fed meeting days were typically the most important macro risk events for equities throughout 2024. This data also reveals an upward shift in relative importance of labor market data starting in the summer of 2024. That's consistent with market concerns about a rising unemployment rate shifting focus away from the inflation side of the Fed's dual mandate.

S&P 500 risk premia on major macro data days show a decline in perceived market risk around inflation

Elections and AI earnings

Another way that we can use daily options data to understand shifts in the relevant equity risk drivers is to simply look at the days with the highest risk scores and then match them to a calendar of macro events. The table below shows the highest implied equity volatility days for 2024 and the associated event. Unsurprisingly, the November election topped the charts and was followed by some of the growth, inflation, and policy days that were plotted in the chart above. This table also highlights an important micro driver of market-level risk in 2024 - three of the top 15 riskiest days for the S&P 500 in 2024 were Nvidia (NVDA) earnings release days.

Elections and AI earnings were key risk drivers in 2024

Single company corporate earnings do not typically influence the overall riskiness of a broadly diversified index, particularly one that contains 500 distinct companies. The ability of Nvidia earnings days to impact overall S&P 500 excess volatility in 2024 provides some insight into two important and related phenomena that, we believe, will be important for risk assets in 2025:

  • Index concentration: The S&P is currently highly concentrated, with the 10 largest companies comprising 38% of the index
  • AI themes: A constellation of outperformers across equity markets have been linked to AI-related themes, like US power demand

What do these views mean for portfolio positioning?

In our tactical liquid alternative portfolios like the BlackRock Tactical Opportunities Fund, we seek to deliver returns that are lowly correlated with stock and bond markets. We use macro data related to growth, inflation, policy and market pricing to seek out long and short investment opportunities across countries and asset classes.

Portfolio positioning shifted underweight US equities relative to non-US equities in the aftermath of the US election, driven primarily by pricing and flows insights. In our view, the elevated concentration of the US equity index in conjunction with unbalanced positioning makes the S&P 500 less attractive than non-US markets on a tactical horizon at the outset of 2025. We have also added to directional short duration positioning in US Treasuries, informed by the market's relatively benign perception of inflation risk despite rising upside risks to the inflation outlook.

This post originally appeared on the BlackRock

data.title: SM Energy: A Look At Its Outlook For 2025
data.description:
SM Energy (NYSE:SM) reported decent Q4 2024 results, with production marginally lower than expected due to third-party crude takeaway issues in the Uinta Basin. SM's outlook for 2025 is a bit better than I expected though, and it may be able to generate nearly $900 million in free cash flow at current strip prices.

This could allow it to put several hundred million dollars towards share repurchases during the year and still reach its leverage target of 1.0x.

There is still a fairly wide production range associated with SM's guidance though, and it also benefits from a planned drawdown of DUCs during the year. Thus, I am keeping my estimate of SM's value at $50 per share for now.

Q4 2024 Results

SM reported 208,000 BOEPD in Q4 2024 production. This was within its expectations for 205,000 to 220,000 BOEPD in production for the quarter, although towards the lower end of that guidance range.

SM's legacy asset production (excluding its Uinta Basin assets) was approximately +1% quarter-over-quarter for total production and -2% quarter-over-quarter for oil production.

SM noted that its Q4 2024 production was reduced by around 3,000 BOEPD due to downtime from third-party crude takeaway, as well as the decision to reject ethane at certain gas processing plants due to better natural gas economics. Northern Oil and Gas (NOG) (SM's Uinta Basin deal partner) mentioned the Uinta Basin crude takeaway issues in its earnings report.

SM reported approximately 36,100 BOEPD in Uinta Basin production in Q4 2024. I had assumed that production would have been 39,000 to 40,000 BOEPD in Q4 2024 after factoring in the impact of delayed wells that was mentioned before.

Without the third-party crude takeaway issues, SM's production would have been fairly in-line with expectations.

SM's Q4 2024 capex was $353.5 million before changes in accruals, which was a bit above its expectations for $320 million to $340 million. SM noted that some costs were accelerated into Q4 2024 due to faster than expected drilling and completions times.

Notes On 2025 Guidance

I had previously modeled SM's 2025 production at 200,000 BOEPD, including 104,000 barrels of oil per day.

This was largely based on SM's June 2024 presentation where it expected approximately 195,000 BOEPD in 2025 production (with around 101,000 to 102,000 barrels of oil per day), but I had assumed that SM was being conservative there.

SM now expects 200,000 to 215,000 BOEPD in 2025 production, including 102,000 to 112,000 barrels of oil per day. This is a relatively wide range in my opinion, with the high-end of its guidance being 7.5% above the low-end of its guidance for total production and 10% above the low-end of its guidance for oil production. The oil production range is a bit higher since SM calculated based it on a 51% to 52% oil cut and its total production guidance range.

SM's total production guidance had a high-end that was 5% above its low-end for 2024 and 4% above its low-end for 2023.

SM is also shifting more development spending to the Uinta Basin for now. In June 2024 it expected around $350 million in 2025 capex for the Uinta Basin, but it is now appearing to allocate in the $450 million to $500 million range there.

SM is temporarily moving money away from South Texas, which had around $500 million earmarked for it before based on the June 2024 slides, but this may be reduced to around $325 million in 2025 capex now.

SM notes that its returns are similar among its three assets at current commodity prices, so the allocation changes are not driven by South Texas returns becoming less competitive.

SM is planning on drilling 105 net wells and completing 150 net wells during 2025, so it is drawing down 45 net DUCs as part of its 2025 program.

2025 Outlook

I am currently modeling SM's 2025 production at 207,000 BOEPD, including 106,000 barrels per day of oil production.

The current strip for 2025 is around $70 WTI oil and $4.25 NYMEX natural gas. At those commodity prices, SM is projected to generate $3.516 billion in revenues before hedges. SM's hedges for 2025 appear to have close to neutral value overall at the moment.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 38,690,000 $69.00 $2,670
NGLs 11,059,500 $24.00 $265
Gas 154,833,000 $3.75 $581
Hedge Value $2
Total $3,518

SM may be able to generate $883 million in free cash flow in 2025 at current strip prices. This includes the impact of $85 million in cash taxes for the year.

$ Million
Lease Operating $408
Transportation $321
Production and Ad Valorem Taxes $196
Cash G&A $135
Net Cash Interest $190
Capex (Excluding Capitalized Interest) $1,300
Cash Taxes $85
Total $2,635

SM's $0.20 per share quarterly fixed dividends adds up to around $92 million in dividends per year.

This would leave $791 million for debt reduction and share repurchases. If SM put all that towards debt reduction, it could reduce its leverage to around 0.8x by the end of the year. It has a leverage target of 1.0x, so there may be room for several hundred million dollars in share repurchases, particularly later in 2025.

Notes On Valuation

I am maintaining my estimate of SM's value at $50 per share based on long-term (after 2025) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. SM's 2025 production guidance is generally a bit higher than what I had modeled before, but the range is fairly wide so I'd like to get further in 2025 to have better confidence about where it will likely end up. This is particularly true for SM's Uinta Basin assets, since SM has only been in control of those assets for a few months so far.

SM is projected to generate approximately $7.70 per share in free cash flow in 2025, although this is with a capex budget that benefits from the drawdown of 45 net DUCs.

Conclusion

SM may be able to generate close to $900 million in 2025 free cash flow at current strip prices. This would allow it to put several hundred million towards share repurchases while still bringing its leverage down to its target of 1.0x.

There is still a decently wide range of expectations for 2025 production though as SM continues to integrate its Uinta Basin assets. I have modeled things a bit on the conservative side and also am keeping my estimate of SM's value at around $50 per share until there is data from more quarters about how its acquired assets are performing.

data.title: FLIN: A Tactical Play On India's Growth With Economic Sensitivity
data.description:
ETF Overview

Franklin FTSE India ETF (NYSEARCA:FLIN) owns a portfolio of nearly 250 Indian equities. The fund tracks the FTSE India Index. FLIN has an expense ratio of 0.19%. This rate is reasonable as it invests in foreign market outside of the U.S. FLIN is tilted towards large-cap growth. The fund has a higher exposure to cyclical sectors, making it sensitive to economic cycles. However, its limited technology exposure may constrain its long-term performance. Despite recent price pullback, its valuation remains above historical average. Therefore, FLIN is better suited as a tactical holding.

Fund Analysis

FLIN is tilted towards large-cap

FLIN tracks the FTSE India Index. This index currently includes about 250 stocks. As can be seen from the chart below, the index has a strong tilt towards large-cap. In fact, large-cap stocks represent 98% of its total portfolio. Normally, large-cap stocks tend to have slower growth profile than mid-cap and small-cap stocks. However, FLIN’s fund style is still tilted towards growth. In fact, growth stocks represent about 59% of its total portfolio. The reason of its tilt towards growth is because India is an emerging market. As we know, emerging markets tend to have better growth prospect than developed markets. Therefore, it is not surprising to see FLIN’s large-cap stocks are also tilted towards growth stocks.

A high exposure to economic sensitive sectors

Now, let us look at FLIN’s sector composition. As can be seen from the table below, cyclical sectors such as basic materials (8.5%), consumer cyclical (11.6%), financial services (25.7%), and real estate (1.4%) sectors accounts for 47.3% of its total portfolio. This means close to half of its portfolio are sensitive to different cycles of the economy. In other words, the fund may outperform when the economy enters an expansion phase, but may underperform in an economic downturn.

FLIN has a lower exposure to defensive sectors

FLIN’s exposure to defensive sectors is quite limited, though. In fact, consumer defensive (6.5%), healthcare (6.2%), and utilities (4.2%) sectors account for only 16.9% of its portfolio. Since defensive sectors typically have slower growth than the broader economy, FLIN’s slightly lower exposure to these sectors may help reduce their drag on the fund’s overall performance. On the other hand, these sectors tend to exhibit lower volatility, especially in economic downturns, FLIN’s lower exposure unfortunately also means the fund may have higher downside risk. Below is a chart that compares FLIN’s downside performance with the S&P 500 index. As can be seen from the chart, the fund has underperformed the S&P 500 index in late 2018 and 2020. Although FLIN has comparable performance in the lengthy correction in 2022, it has underperformed in the recent correction since late 2024.

FLIN has trailed the S&P 500 index since its inception

Since we have just compared FLIN’s downside risk to the S&P 500 index, reader may ask what about FLIN’s long-term return relative to the S&P 500 index? Let us take a look at FLIN's performance relative to the S&P 500 index. As can be seen from the chart below, the fund has delivered a total return of only 60.2% since its inception in 2018 while the S&P 500 index delivered much superior total return of 167.4%.

FLIN’s fund price is sensitive to currency exchange rate

Why has FLIN underperformed the S&P 500 index despite its strong tilt towards growth? Here, we will highlight two primary reasons. First, FLIN’s fund price can be impacted by the strength and weakness of the U.S. dollar. In fact, as can be seen from the chart below, it has an inverse correlation to the strength of the U.S. dollar. Since equities in emerging markets tend to be more volatile, a small change in the strength and weakness of U.S. dollar can cause a big swing in FLIN’s fund price. As the chart below shows, The Indian Rupee has depreciated by 27.6% since FLIN’s inception in 2018. Had the currency rate not changed in the past 7 years, FLIN’s total return would have been at least 85% or higher instead of the current return of 60.2%.

FLIN’s exposure to technology is limited

Another reason to FLIN’s underperformance is its limited exposure to technology. In the sector allocation chart we have shown earlier, technology sector only represents about 13.3% of FLIN’s portfolio. In contrast, the sector represents nearly one-third of the S&P 500 index. As we know, technology sector is one of the fastest growing sectors among all other sectors in the past and is expected to continue to deliver strong growth thanks to many technological megatrends. Therefore, FLIN’s limited exposure has hindered its performance and weakened its growth outlook.

Earnings growth rate expected to accelerate

Fortunately, FLIN’s earnings growth rate is expected to accelerate in 2026. We will illustrate this using consensus estimate of stocks in the MSCI India Index. Although this index is not the same as the FTSE India Index that FLIN tracks, there are significant overlaps, and both have similar returns in the past. Therefore, it will give us good idea of the growth expectations of FLIN’s portfolio. As can be seen from the chart below, consensus earnings growth rate is expected to jump from only 6.4% last year to 17.2% this year. Therefore, near-term growth appears to be quite solid.

Valuation is now more reasonable

Now let us evaluate FLIN’s valuation, although recent pullback in FLIN’s fund price has resulted in a lower valuation, the current forward P/E ratio of 21.1x of MSCI India Index is still above the historical average, suggesting that FLIN’s valuation is still not attractive enough.

Investor Takeaway

FLIN’s strong tilt towards large-cap growth stocks makes it a compelling option for exposure to India’s expanding economy. However, its high sensitivity to economic cycles, currency fluctuations, and limited technology exposure have contributed to its underperformance against the S&P 500. Therefore, FLIN should be treated as a tactical holding rather than a core holding in one’s portfolio.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: Big 4 Private Equity Firms In Brighter Mood; Falling Investment In Biotech
data.description:
Surging revenue from asset management businesses appeared to lift the spirits of private equity's Big Four firms, which all set quarterly or annual records for fourth quarter 2024 fee-related earnings.

The average sentiment score for the most recent batch of earnings calls by Blackstone Inc. (BX), Apollo Global Management Inc. (APG), KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) and The Carlyle Group Inc. (CG) nudged to its highest level in several years, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of the language used by executives and analysts on the call. Another likely factor in the brighter tone was a broadly shared expectation of more M&A and IPO activity in 2025, which would give a boost to their private equity strategies.

Exits, in particular, are a top priority across the private equity industry. The middle market drove the increase in the monetization of portfolio company investments in 2024. Could the Big Four be more active in 2025?

Blackstone President Jonathan Gray said the outlook looks good.

"The environment is clearly here getting better," Gray said on the firm's Jan. 30 earnings call, citing a strong economy and healthy equity markets. "We think large, profitable companies can go public."

Chart Of The Week: Biotech investments decline for 3rd consecutive year

⮞ Private equity- and venture capital-backed transactions in biotechnology declined for the third consecutive year in 2024 to $20.28 billion globally, down 11% from $22.79 billion in 2023, according to Market Intelligence data.

⮞ Deal values increased every quarter of 2024, indicating signs of a potential reversal.

⮞ Within the larger healthcare sector, biotechnology absorbed more private investment in 2024 than any other industry, except for pharmaceuticals.

Top Deals

- KKR & Co. Inc. completed the second stage of its two-tranche tender offer for the common shares and share options of Fuji Soft Inc. KKR now owns nearly 58% interest in Fuji Soft and will proceed to take the Japanese software company private.

- Funds managed by KKR will acquire a stake in Sydney-based employment management platform Employment Hero Pty. Ltd. from Seek Investments Pty. Ltd. The deal is expected to close by the first quarter.

- The Carlyle Group Inc. made an additional investment in distributed generation platform Aspen Power Partners LLC. Carlyle made its initial commitment to Aspen Power in 2022.

Fundraising Update

- GTCR LLC raised about $3.6 billion in total commitments for Strategic Growth Fund II at final close. The vehicle will invest in lower-middle-market and midmarket companies.

- Greenfield Partners LLC secured $400 million in capital commitments primarily for its Fund III. The vehicle will invest in early growth technology companies.

- Madison River Capital LLC raised more than $370 million in capital commitments at the final close of its inaugural fund. Madison River Capital Fund I will focus on control buyouts of lower-middle-market companies in the healthcare services, industrial and business services sectors.

- Invalda INVL raised €305 million at the first close of its second private equity fund. INVL Private Equity Fund II will invest in businesses across the Baltics, Poland, Romania and the broader EU.

Middle-Market Highlights

- HealthEdge Investment Partners LLC closed the sale of patient-monitoring-device maker LifeSync Corp. The buyer was Amphenol Corp. (APH).

- Grovecourt Capital Partners purchased IMRIS Inc. (OTC:IMRSQ), which provides magnetic resonance imaging solutions. Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) sold the company.

- Kinzie Capital Partners LP added Fraser Steel, which makes steel tubular products, to its portfolio.

Focus On: Private Equity Fundraising

Private equity fundraising struggled in 2024, with total capital raised declining for the third consecutive year.

Private equity firms also took longer to close funds. Investment vehicles that closed in 2024 were open for a record 21.9 months versus 19.6 months in 2023 and 14.1 months in 2018, according to a report from McKinsey & Co.

Fundraising for buyout, growth equity and venture capital each fell as much as 25% compared with 2023. However, fundraising of midmarket vehicles - those ranging from $1 billion to $5 billion in size - remained flat year over year, according to McKinsey.

Original Post
